channel.title: q-fin.CP updates on arXiv.org
channel.description: q-fin.CP updates on the arXiv.org e-print archive.

item.title: Shapley in Context: Explaining Financial Language with Domain Expertise
item.description: arXiv:2607.00856v1 Announce Type: new Abstract: In recent years, large language models have achieved remarkable success and have seen growing adoption in financial applications. At the same time, explainability remains critical in finance, a domain characterized by high stakes and strict regulatory requirements. Although numerous methods have been proposed to explain black box machine learning models, the majority of these approaches are designed for general purpose tasks and do not incorporate domain specific knowledge. In this work, we study the explainability of financial textual data modeled by large language models through the lens of the Shapley value. Specifically, we investigate whether Shapley based attributions align with established financial domain knowledge. Through rigorous theoretical analysis and extensive empirical evaluations, we demonstrate that Shapley values can yield explanations that are consistent with financial reasoning and can offer meaningful insights into the model's behavior in text based financial applications.
item.dc:creator: Dangxing Chen, Pengzhan Guo

item.title: ASRI: An Aggregated Systemic Risk Index for Cryptocurrency Markets
item.description: arXiv:2602.03874v3 Announce Type: replace-cross Abstract: The Aggregated Systemic Risk Index (ASRI) is an interpretable, channel-decomposed measure of crypto-native systemic stress, aggregating four transmission channels -- stablecoin concentration, DeFi liquidity, cross-market contagion, and regulatory opacity -- into a single auditable composite. Methodologically it belongs to the financial-network tradition of connectedness and contagion measurement: the contagion channel is a cross-market connectedness construct, and a Diebold-Yilmaz connectedness series computed on the sub-indices serves as the network benchmark. We evaluate it retrospectively against four crypto crises (Terra/Luna, Celsius/3AC, FTX, SVB); it is not a validated real-time early-warning system. Under autocorrelation-robust (HAC) inference referenced to a finite-sample fixed-$b$ distribution, the three large-signal events are only borderline at the 5% level ($p \approx 0.04$--$0.06$, none surviving Bonferroni); Terra/Luna is clearly non-significant. On a fair-baseline comparison on identical labels, ASRI's day-level discrimination (AUROC 0.866) is indistinguishable on marginal bootstrap intervals from its strongest single channel (Contagion, 0.851) and from the first principal component of its channels (0.858); a paired bootstrap separates them only by a small, structural margin ($\leq 0.016$ AUROC, absent in AUPRC) reflecting ASRI nesting those baselines, not a discriminative gain, and a standalone equity-volatility (VIX) series matches ASRI (0.875). We read the value of aggregation as interpretive -- channel attribution, lead-time, and regime structure in one transparent, reproducible composite -- rather than as superior discrimination, alongside a methodological account of how robust inference reshapes crisis-detection claims.
item.dc:creator: Murad Farzulla, Andrew Maksakov
